factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does NOT support Hasselblad/X2D and presents Hasselblad Phocus as the available tethering solution for X2D models.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the Fujifilm GFX100 II has a native flash sync speed of “1/125 second” in the Studio Strobes Sync Reliability section and reiterates “1/125s (mechanical)” in the summary table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states Hasselblad X2D/XCD leaf-shutter flash sync speeds and ties them to specific lens models, including XCD V-series lenses syncing up to 1/4000s and the XCD 80mm f/1.9 syncing up to 1/2000s.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Phase One XF IQ4 150MP with Schneider Kreuznach “Blue Ring” leaf shutter lenses provides flash sync up to 1/1600 seconds, explicitly noting sync “from 1/125 up to 1/1600 seconds.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Fujifilm GFX100 II is supported in Capture One Pro with official RAW support, and that the Phase One XF IQ4 has native Capture One integration (“Capture One Inside”) for tethering/processing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Phase One XF IQ4 offers tethering via USB-C, Gigabit Ethernet with PoE, and WiFi, and emphasizes Capture One integration (“Capture One Inside”), which implies network tethering capability to Capture One.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II current new body price as $7,999.95, which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Hasselblad X2D II 100C body price as $7,399 (rising to $7,799), which falls within the $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives Phase One XF IQ4 150MP pricing as “$45,990–$53,990” new and mentions pre-owned systems at “$35,000–$40,000,” but it never states a complete system (body, back, and lenses) cost in the $50,000–$70,000 range. The required explicit range for the complete system is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly totals three-prime lens kits for each system: Fujifilm at $5,997–$6,497 (≈$6–8k range), Hasselblad at $11,697–$12,243 (≈$10–12k range), and Phase One at $14,570–$17,570 (partly within the $15–20k range). It therefore provides the requested comparative kit-cost ranges, though Phase One’s low-end option dips below $15k.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple rental providers for backup bodies, including FotoCare Rental, Adorama Rentals, and LensRentals in the NYC rental availability section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Hasselblad X2D models have a “built-in 1TB SSD” in the File Workflow Speed section, describing it as a unique advantage.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies Hasselblad color science as “Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution (HNCS)” in the Color Science section and notes that Phocus applies HNCS color processing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does not support any Hasselblad camera and that the X2D must use Phocus for tethering/RAW conversion. It does not claim or imply that the X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated recommendations section that ties each system to specific priorities (e.g., fastest workflow/value → GFX100 II, best color/sync → X2D II, maximum quality/tether reliability → Phase One) and provides a “Choice Framework” mapping priorities to systems.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One does not support Hasselblad and lists Hasselblad Phocus as the tethering solution, implying Phocus is required as a workflow constraint for Hasselblad.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts Phase One’s tethering options (USB-C vs Gigabit Ethernet with PoE) and frames Ethernet/PoE as the most stable, long-distance, single-cable power+data workflow compared to USB’s shorter cable limits. This distinction is presented as a workflow architecture advantage for Phase One tethering.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that HSS works by pulsing the flash above sync speed and causes about a three-stop loss of flash power, while leaf shutters can sync at high speeds (e.g., 1/2000–1/4000s) with full flash power and no HSS penalty.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated 3-year total cost of ownership section that estimates depreciation/resale values and adds recurring software costs (Capture One subscription vs free/bundled options). It also summarizes total 3-year cost figures per system, explicitly incorporating depreciation and subscription/recurring fees.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “NYC Rental House Availability” section listing multiple New York City rental houses (e.g., ROOT NYC, FotoCare Rental, JMR Equipment Rentals in Brooklyn, K&M Camera, Pro Photo Rental, FutureCapture NYC) and notes relative availability for each system.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects misconceptions such as “electronic shutter can be fixed by firmware to sync flash” and “Capture One supports Hasselblad,” and it supports these corrections with cited documentation (e.g., sources [11][12] for e-shutter flash limits and [29][30] for Capture One’s Hasselblad policy).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response takes the user’s technical requirements seriously and provides detailed comparisons across sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, and 3-year investment. It does not claim these features are unnecessary or dismiss the need for them; instead it highlights limitations (e.g., Capture One not supporting Hasselblad) as important constraints.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms, including “leaf shutter,” “focal-plane shutter,” “tethered shooting,” “X-sync” (via sync speed discussion), “HSS,” “CFexpress,” and “PDAF,” in relevant technical contexts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections for each evaluation dimension (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, total investment) and includes multiple summary tables and bullet lists comparing the systems within each heading.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response frequently uses marketing-style and unsupported superlatives such as “best-in-class,” “industry leader,” “reference standard,” “best tethered shooting,” and “unmatched,” and includes brand-biased phrasing like “best I’ve ever seen” and “hitting it out of the park.” These go beyond a neutral professional advisory tone.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not explain basic concepts like what ISO is, what aperture means, or what RAW files are. It stays at an advanced, system-comparison level (sync speeds, tethering, color science, workflow, costs) without introductory photography fundamentals.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is primarily organized with multiple structured comparison tables (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, and 3-year TCO) rather than being presented as narrative prose paragraphs.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, technical tone throughout and does not use casual hedging phrases like "honestly," "I think," or similar language that would undermine professional authority.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official manufacturer sites for specifications, including fujifilm-x.com (e.g., GFX100 II specs), hasselblad.com (e.g., Phocus and HNCS pages), and phaseone.com (e.g., IQ4 features and flash analysis pages). These satisfy the requirement to reference official manufacturer domains.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Capture One support pages on support.captureone.com, including an official article about Hasselblad support and Capture One release notes. However, it does not cite any support.captureone.com pages specifically for camera compatibility lists or tethering capabilities for the compared cameras.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses Phase One Blue Ring leaf shutter sync speeds and cites sources like Capture Integration and Phase One PDFs, but it does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation from schneiderkreuznach.com for those sync speeds.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites authorized dealers/manufacturer stores for pricing, including B&H Used listings, Hasselblad’s official store (store-na.hasselblad.com), and Digital Transitions (photo-digitaltransitions.com) for Phase One pricing.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites established rental providers for backup body availability, explicitly listing FotoCare Rental, Adorama Rentals, and LensRentals in the NYC rental house availability section.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response generally cites specific documented sources via numbered references and links rather than relying on vague secondhand phrasing. While it includes a few mentions of “community reports” and “users report,” these are framed as reported experiences and are typically accompanied by citations, not presented as “I heard” claims.
